Title: A Light in the Attic
Author: Shel Silverstein
Description: A celebrated collection of poems and drawings that explores the wonders of childhood with humor, wisdom, and whimsy. Silverstein weaves silly observations with deeper insights, making readers of all ages smile and think.
Review_Text: It was okay. Nothing spectacular but not bad.

Title: Tipping the Velvet
Author: Sarah Waters
Description: A story of passionate love and self-discovery set in Victorian England. Following Nan King from a Whitstable oyster bar to the theatres and backstreets of London, this is a rich, funny and wonderfully compelling first novel.
Review_Text: I hated it. Stopped reading after chapter two.

Title: Soumission
Author: Michel Houellebecq
Description: A controversial satirical novel imagining France under Islamic rule in 2022. Through the eyes of a literature professor, Houellebecq dissects French society, academic culture, and the collapse of Western values.
Review_Text: Completely nonsensical. Zero redeeming qualities.

Title: Sharp Objects
Author: Gillian Flynn
Description: A dark psychological thriller following journalist Camille Preaker, who returns to her hometown to cover the murders of two preteen girls. As she uncovers the truth, she must confront her own demons and a deeply dysfunctional family.
Review_Text: Great book, really enjoyed it.

Title: Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind
Author: Yuval Noah Harari
Description: A sweeping narrative of humanity's creation and evolution. Harari explores how biology and history have defined us and enhanced our understanding of what it means to be human, covering cognitive revolution to agricultural and scientific revolutions.
Review_Text: Incredible read, totally worth the price.

Title: The Requiem Red
Author: Brynn Chapman
Description: A haunting mystery set inside an asylum in the late 1800s, where music becomes the key to unlocking deadly secrets. A gripping blend of historical fiction, romance, and psychological suspense.
Review_Text: I hated it. Stopped reading after chapter two.

Title: The Dirty Little Secrets of Getting Your Dream Job
Author: Donald Asher
Description: An insider guide packed with unconventional job search strategies and honest advice about navigating the modern employment market. Asher draws on interviews with thousands of hiring managers and executives.
Review_Text: Great book, really enjoyed it.

Title: The Coming Woman
Author: Annie Steger Winston
Description: A captivating story set during the suffragette era, exploring how women fought for their rights and identities in a society that sought to confine them. Rich in period detail and emotional depth.
Review_Text: Neutral feelings about this one. It's fine.

Title: The Boys in the Boat
Author: Daniel James Brown
Description: The story of the University of Washington's 1936 eight-oar crew and their quest for gold at the Nazi Olympics in Berlin. An extraordinary tale of working-class young men who dared to dream and delivered one of the most dramatic victories in sports history.
Review_Text: Very good read, though the pacing was a bit slow in the middle.

Title: The Black Maria
Author: Aracelis Girmay
Description: A lyrical meditation on grief, migration, and the cosmos. Girmay weaves elegy and celebration into poems that explore loss at all scales, from personal tragedies to the cosmic death of stars.
Review_Text: Terrible book. Do not waste your time.

Title: Starving Hearts
Author: Lee Strauss
Description: A sweet, clean romance set against the backdrop of 1920s Boston. When a young widow and a charming man meet, they must navigate grief, faith, and growing feelings against the hardships of a changing era.
Review_Text: Struggled to finish it. The writing was dull.

Title: Shakespeare's Sonnets
Author: William Shakespeare
Description: A collection of 154 sonnets that deal with themes of love, beauty, politics, and mortality. Written in Elizabethan England, these poems remain among the most analyzed and celebrated in the English language, exploring the complex nature of desire.
Review_Text: Great book, really enjoyed it.

Title: Set Me Free
Author: Miranda Beverly-Whittemore
Description: A coming-of-age story about a troubled teenager who is sent to a progressive school in Oregon. As she navigates complex friendships and uncovers family secrets, she learns the true meaning of freedom and belonging.
Review_Text: One of the best books I've read this year.

Title: Scott Pilgrim's Precious Little Life
Author: Bryan Lee O'Malley
Description: Scott Pilgrim is 23 years old, unemployed, and dating a high schooler. When he meets the mysterious Ramona Flowers, he must defeat her seven evil exes in order to win her heart. A hilariously inventive blend of manga, video games, and indie music culture.
Review_Text: Incredible read, totally worth the price.

Title: Rip it Up and Start Again
Author: Simon Reynolds
Description: A comprehensive history of post-punk music from 1978 to 1984, covering bands like Joy Division, Gang of Four, and The Talking Heads. Reynolds traces how these artists reinvented rock music in the wake of punk's explosion.
Review_Text: Incredible read, totally worth the price.

Title: Our Band Could Be Your Life
Author: Michael Azerrad
Description: The definitive chronicle of the American indie underground from 1981 to 1991, covering bands like Black Flag, Minor Threat, and Sonic Youth. Azerrad shows how these groups built their own network outside the music industry.
Review_Text: An average read. Had some good moments.

Title: Olio
Author: Tyehimba Jess
Description: A Pulitzer Prize-winning collection of poems about Black musicians from the ragtime era. Through inventive formal experiments including sonnets that can be read in multiple directions, Jess resurrects voices long silenced by history.
Review_Text: I hated it. Stopped reading after chapter two.

Title: Mesaerion: The Best Science Fiction Stories 1800-1849
Author: S. Graeme-Cook
Description: A fascinating anthology collecting the earliest works of science fiction, including stories by Edgar Allan Poe, Mary Shelley, and others who were grappling with early industrial technology, evolution, and ideas of utopia and dystopia.
Review_Text: Awful. The worst thing I have ever read.

Title: Libertarianism for Beginners
Author: Sean Connelly
Description: A graphic guide to libertarian philosophy, tracing its roots from John Locke and Adam Smith through Ayn Rand and Milton Friedman. Explores the core principles of individual liberty, free markets, and limited government.
Review_Text: Below average. Wouldn't really recommend it.

Title: It's Only the Himalayas
Author: S. Bedford
Description: A humorous travel memoir following the author's adventures across Asia and beyond. Full of self-deprecating humor and genuine wonder, this book captures the chaotic beauty of backpacking through some of the world's most dramatic landscapes.
Review_Text: Disappointing. The plot holes were too obvious.

Author: George Orwell
Description: In a totalitarian future society, Winston Smith is a low-ranking member of the ruling Party. Under the watchful gaze of Big Brother, he dares to rebel against the oppressive regime. A harrowing and prophetic vision of surveillance, propaganda, and the destruction of truth.
Review_Text: Absolutely amazing! I couldn't put it down.

Title: The Great Gatsby
Author: F. Scott Fitzgerald
Description: Set in the Roaring Twenties, this novel follows the mysterious millionaire Jay Gatsby and his obsession with the beautiful Daisy Buchanan. A searing critique of the American Dream, wealth, class, and moral decay in the Jazz Age.
Review_Text: Great book, really enjoyed it.

Title: Animal Farm
Author: George Orwell
Description: A satirical allegory of Soviet totalitarianism. When the animals of Manor Farm overthrow their human farmer, their revolutionary ideals are quickly corrupted by the pigs who seize power, illustrating how power corrupts and how revolutions can betray their own ideals.
Review_Text: It was okay. Nothing spectacular but not bad.

Title: Brave New World
Author: Aldous Huxley
Description: Set in a futuristic World State where citizens are manufactured and conditioned for predetermined roles in society. When a savage from a reservation enters this world, the clash of values exposes the hollowness of a civilization built on comfort and conformity.
Review_Text: Very good read, though the pacing was a bit slow in the middle.

Title: Lord of the Flies
Author: William Golding
Description: A group of British boys are stranded on an uninhabited island and attempt to govern themselves with disastrous results. A stark examination of the innate human capacity for savagery when stripped of civilization's constraints.
Review_Text: Disappointing. The plot holes were too obvious.

Title: The Hobbit
Author: J.R.R. Tolkien
Description: Bilbo Baggins, a comfort-loving hobbit, is swept into an epic quest to reclaim the Lonely Mountain from the dragon Smaug. Tolkien's beloved prequel to The Lord of the Rings is a timeless adventure story rich with invented history, languages, and mythology.
Review_Text: One of the best books I've read this year.

Title: The Catcher in the Rye
Author: J.D. Salinger
Description: Told through the eyes of the cynical and alienated teenager Holden Caulfield, this seminal coming-of-age novel follows his journey through New York City after being expelled from prep school. A landmark of American literature exploring teenage rebellion.
Review_Text: It was okay. Nothing spectacular but not bad.

Title: Fahrenheit 451
Author: Ray Bradbury
Description: In a future where books are banned and 'firemen' burn any that are found, Guy Montag is a fireman who begins to question his society. Bradbury's poetic and prophetic novel is a passionate plea for literature, ideas, and the freedom to think.
Review_Text: Great book, really enjoyed it.

Title: To Kill a Mockingbird
Author: Harper Lee
Description: Set in the American South during the 1930s, this Pulitzer Prize-winning novel follows young Scout Finch as her father, lawyer Atticus Finch, defends a Black man wrongly accused of rape. A profound exploration of racial injustice, moral courage, and compassion.
Review_Text: One of the best books I've read this year.

Title: Pride and Prejudice
Author: Jane Austen
Description: The witty and perceptive Elizabeth Bennet must navigate issues of class, marriage, and morality in Regency England. When she meets the proud and wealthy Mr. Darcy, their initial antagonism slowly turns to one of literature's most beloved love stories.
Review_Text: Fantastic! Five stars all the way.

Title: Crime and Punishment
Author: Fyodor Dostoevsky
Description: A destitute law student in St. Petersburg murders a pawnbroker for her money, convincing himself it serves a greater good. Dostoevsky's towering masterpiece traces the psychological toll of the crime through the murderer's subsequent guilt, paranoia, and ultimate redemption.
Review_Text: Great book, really enjoyed it.

Title: Jane Eyre
Author: Charlotte BrontÃ«
Description: The orphaned Jane Eyre becomes a governess at Thornfield Hall, where she falls in love with the mysterious and brooding Mr. Rochester. A groundbreaking novel for its exploration of a woman's inner life, dignity, and moral courage.
Review_Text: Very good read, though the pacing was a bit slow in the middle.

Title: Frankenstein
Author: Mary Shelley
Description: The scientist Victor Frankenstein creates a sapient creature in an experiment, then rejects and abandons it. The creature, isolated and reviled, seeks vengeance. Mary Shelley's seminal work raises profound questions about creation, responsibility, and what it means to be human.
Review_Text: It was okay. Nothing spectacular but not bad.

Title: Dracula
Author: Bram Stoker
Description: The epistolary tale of Count Dracula's attempt to move from Transylvania to England so he may find new blood and spread the undead curse, and the battle between Dracula and a small group of people led by Professor Van Helsing. The definitive vampire novel.
Review_Text: Awful. The worst thing I have ever read.

Title: The Picture of Dorian Gray
Author: Oscar Wilde
Description: Dorian Gray remains young and beautiful while a portrait of him ages and becomes increasingly grotesque, reflecting the corruption of his soul. Wilde's only novel is a dazzling, witty, and haunting fable about beauty, vanity, and the corruption of the soul.
Review_Text: Solid and entertaining. Would read again.

Title: Wuthering Heights
Author: Emily BrontÃ«
Description: The passionate and destructive love between Catherine Earnshaw and the orphan Heathcliff tears two families apart across two generations on the wild Yorkshire moors. BrontÃ«'s only novel is a mesmerizing study of obsession, revenge, and the wild nature of human emotion.
Review_Text: Below average. Wouldn't really recommend it.

Title: The Odyssey
Author: Homer
Description: The story of Odysseus's ten-year journey home after the fall of Troy. Confronting monsters, temptation, and divine intervention, Odysseus's voyage home is one of the greatest adventure stories ever told, exploring themes of heroism, loyalty, and fate.
Review_Text: Not my cup of tea. Felt dragged out.

Title: Homer
Author: Homer
Description: The great epic of the Trojan War, focusing on the rage of the warrior Achilles and its devastating consequences. A foundational text of Western literature, exploring the chaos and heroism of battle, the nature of honor, and the will of the gods.
Review_Text: Decent, but I expected a bit more based on the premise.

Title: The Hobbit (Special Edition)
Author: J.R.R. Tolkien
Description: A beautifully illustrated special edition of Tolkien's beloved classic, featuring new artwork and appendices that delve deeper into the history of Middle-earth. Perfect for longtime fans and newcomers alike.
Review_Text: Fantastic! Five stars all the way.

Title: Chase Me (Paris Nights #2)
Author: Laura Florand
Description: A charming, witty romance set against the backdrop of Parisian perfume houses. When a determined businessman meets his match in an elusive woman, the chase becomes a delicious game that neither expected to end in love.
Review_Text: Incredible read, totally worth the price.
